questions.questionText: Rigor mortis starts to appear after death at approximately:
questions.optionB: 1-2 hours (starts in small muscles — eyelids/jaw, becomes fully established in 12 hours, passes off in 24-36 hours)
questions.explanation: Rigor mortis: stiffening of muscles after death due to ATP depletion → actin-myosin bond cannot be broken. Nysten's rule: starts in small muscles (eyelids, jaw, face) → progresses to larger muscles (extremities) → disappears in same order. Onset: 1-2 hrs, fully established: 12 hrs, passes off: 24-36 hrs (due to decomposition/autolysis). Factors hastening: high ambient temperature, prior exercise, fever, small body. Cadaveric spasm: instantaneous rigor at time of death (ante-mortem activity — clutching weapon). Reference: Reddy — Rigor Mortis.

questions.questionText: Livor mortis (postmortem lividity) is caused by:
questions.optionB: Gravitational settling of blood in dependent parts of the body after cessation of circulation
questions.explanation: Livor mortis (hypostasis/postmortem lividity): gravitational pooling of blood in dependent (lowest) parts of body. Appears: 1-2 hours after death as reddish-purple discoloration. Fixed: after 6-8 hours (blood extravasates from vessels into tissues). Becomes non-blanchable when fixed. Helps determine: time since death, position of body after death, posture change (dual lividity). Color: cherry-red (CO poisoning, cold), brown (methemoglobinemia), dark blue (asphyxia). Reference: Reddy — Livor Mortis.

questions.optionB: Cooling of the body after death (body temperature falls to reach ambient temperature)
questions.explanation: Algor mortis: post-mortem cooling of body. Body loses heat → approaches ambient temperature. Rate: ~1.5°F (0.83°C) per hour in first 12 hours under standard conditions (Newton's law of cooling — modified). Factors affecting: initial body temperature, ambient temperature, body size, clothing, wind, water. Used to estimate time since death. Rectal/liver temperature most reliable. Initial plateau: body may not cool for 1-2 hours (thermal equilibrium/post-mortem caloricity). Reference: Reddy — Algor Mortis.

questions.questionText: Autopsy (post-mortem examination) is mandatory in which type of death?
questions.optionB: Medico-legal cases (suspicious, unnatural, sudden unexpected death, death in custody, dowry death within 7 years)
questions.explanation: Medico-legal autopsy: mandatory in deaths that are suspicious, unnatural, sudden/unexpected, deaths in custody, alleged dowry deaths (Sec 174 CrPC), deaths due to accident/suicide/homicide, unidentified bodies. Ordered by: police/magistrate. Pathological/clinical autopsy: to determine cause of death in natural disease — requires consent from relatives. Autopsy surgeon: should ideally be 2 doctors (minimum 1 RMP). Section 174 CrPC: police inquest. Section 176 CrPC: magistrate inquest (deaths in custody). Reference: Reddy — Autopsy.

questions.questionText: Adipocere formation is a type of modification of decomposition that involves:
questions.optionB: Conversion of body fat into a waxy soap-like substance (saponification) in warm, moist, anaerobic conditions
questions.explanation: Adipocere (saponification): body fat → waxy, greasy, yellowish-white substance (fatty acids — palmitic, stearic, oleic). Occurs in warm, moist, anaerobic conditions (bodies in water, damp soil). Helps preserve body shape and features. Lecithinase from Clostridium welchii (C. perfringens) accelerates the process. Time: begins in weeks, fully formed in months. Mummification: dry, hot, well-ventilated conditions → desiccation. Both are modifications that slow decomposition and help preserve body for identification. Reference: Reddy — Adipocere.
